title: Use soft regime gating instead of hard splits for dry, wet, and track-energy modes
summary: Mixture-of-experts models are designed for nonstationary dynamics, and pit-stop logic is visibly regime-dependent: wet/intermediate races, abrasive tracks, sprint weekends, and endgame laps obey different stop incentives.
rationale: Mixture-of-experts models are designed for nonstationary dynamics, and pit-stop logic is visibly regime-dependent: wet/intermediate races, abrasive tracks, sprint weekends, and endgame laps obey different stop incentives. A soft gate can learn those mode boundaries more flexibly than a single global model or brittle manual splits.
implementation_hint: Train 3-4 specialists, for example `dry slick`, `wet/intermediate`, `high-energy track`, and `late-race endgame`. Use a simple gating model on causal context (`RaceProgress`, compound class, absolute weekend compound hardness, estimated laps remaining, year/race metadata) and output a weighted average probability.
expected_effect: Should capture heterogeneity that a single model smooths away, especially when the same tyre-age signal means different things across regimes.
risk: Only 26 races means the gate can overfit if too many experts are created; start with very few broad regimes.
